items.fight_news: Strickland's upright stance and parry-heavy boxing leave his hips exposed to Chimaev's explosive double-leg entries from the outside. The defensive metrics show Strickland struggles most when forced backward quickly.

Chimaev's cardio in five-round fights has historically dipped after round two. But, he did well against DDP. If Strickland survives the early onslaught, his jab and teep kicks become massive weapons that can dictate the championship rounds.
Strickland's cage walk efficiency is elite. He never gets trapped on the fence willingly. However, Chimaev covers distance faster than anyone at 185, bypassing standard footwork defense entirely.

The first 90 seconds dictate the live betting lines. If Chimaev secures top control early without expending his entire gas tank, live odds will swing massively in his favor based on historical control time data.

Van's counter-striking against grappling entries is his primary defense. He looks to catch opponents changing levels rather than sprawling defensively, which creates high-risk but high-reward exchanges.

Taira uses an elite back-take system off body locks. If he forces Van into the clinch against the fence, his transition to the back is arguably the fastest and most lethal in the flyweight division today.

The championship rounds are a major variable. Van has proven 25-minute championship pace, while Taira's pacing in late rounds of high-volume striking affairs remains a slight unknown at this elite level.

bouts.title: Alexander Volkov vs. Waldo Cortes Acosta

items.fight_news: Volkov's teep kick to the body is structurally designed to punish boxers who lean heavily on their lead leg, perfectly targeting Cortes Acosta's preferred striking stance.
Cortes Acosta must close distance rapidly, but Volkov's lateral movement has improved significantly in his late career, denying the straight-line entries that his opponent relies on.
In a smaller cage, Cortes Acosta would have a distinct edge for dirty boxing, but the larger Octagon at the Prudential Center strongly favors Volkov's outside kicking game and range management.
Brady's chain wrestling against the fence is relentless, but Buckley's explosive hip escapes make him incredibly difficult to hold down for prolonged periods without risking a reversal.
Buckley's tendency to throw leaping, power-heavy strikes creates massive pockets of space for Brady to time reactive double legs underneath the punches.
If Brady cannot secure early takedowns, his striking defense tends to rely on a high guard, leaving his midsection wide open to Buckley's vicious body kicks.
Green relies on his shoulder roll and head movement, which is highly effective against straight punches but traditionally vulnerable to the chopping calf kicks Stephens consistently employs.
Stephens loads up heavily on his overhand right. Green's entire defensive system is built on pulling back from exactly this type of committed, looping strike to land quick, piercing counters.

The pacing heavily favors Green if the fight extends past the first round. Stephens' power output requires significant oxygen, while Green's volume is steady, economical, and designed to drown opponents late.
Gautier's high finish rate is heavily reliant on his right cross, but Diaz's tendency to slip outside the lead foot nullifies straight power punches effectively.
The clinch exchanges will be the deciding tactical factor here; Diaz's defensive underhooks must be perfect to stop Gautier from dominating the dirty boxing range.
Amosov's elite Sambo background translates to suffocating top pressure, but Alvarez is one of the few welterweights who actively hunts high-percentage submissions off his back.
Alvarez's physical length creates sweeping leverage that Amosov rarely faces. If Amosov cannot pass the guard quickly, he risks being caught in triangle or armbar traps.

Dawson's offensive wrestling metrics are top-tier, but his striking defense drops significantly when he is forced to retreat. Rebecki needs to use his heavy hands to back Dawson against the cage early.
Rebecki's judo base makes traditional single and double-leg entries difficult to finish; Dawson will likely need to rely on back-takes from the clinch rather than standard shots in the open.

Miller's veteran timing on the guillotine choke is a specific hazard for Gordon's blast-double entries. Gordon must use single-leg variations or upper-body clinches to avoid Miller's front headlock traps.
Gordon's path to victory lies in sheer volume and pace. If he can survive the first-round submission threat, his cardio metrics suggest he can outwork Miller in rounds two and three.
Kopylov's southpaw liver kick is the statistically highest-damage strike in this matchup. Tulio tends to shell up high under pressure, leaving his midsection vulnerable to this exact weapon.
Tulio must disrupt Kopylov's rhythm with forward pressure. If Kopylov is allowed to set his feet at kicking range, the statistical probability of a body-kick finish rises drastically.
Gomis utilizes excellent lateral movement to keep fights in open space, directly countering Sabatini's reliance on chain-wrestling against the fence.
Sabatini cannot afford to stand at range. He must eat kicks to close the distance, relying on his durability to secure the body lock where his grappling advantage becomes overwhelming.

Susurkaev's wrestling pacing is relentless, but Santos possesses exceptional explosive hip escapes. The crucial metric is whether Santos can break grips within the first 10 seconds of being taken down.
Santos carries legitimate knockout power, but over-committing on strikes will give Susurkaev the exact timing he needs for reactive level changes.
Carpenter's scrambling ability often masks his defensive striking deficiencies. Ochoa's optimal path to victory is utilizing heavy low kicks to compromise Carpenter's explosive takedown entries.
If the fight goes to the mat, Carpenter's top control is stifling, meaning Ochoa must prioritize scrambling to his feet immediately rather than playing guard.
